# Excerpt cards for `/polish`

These are **short anchored excerpts** from the exemplars. They are reminders of tone and structure, not reusable text blocks.

---

## Exemplar 1 — White 2012

### Excerpt 1a — Early conceptual framing
> "The intracellular recycling function of autophagy has a varying impact on cellular and organismal physiology that depends on the circumstances."

The paper's central problem (context-dependence) is announced as a plain empirical observation, not a rhetorical claim. The word "circumstances" does the work that other writers would spread across a paragraph of justification.

### Excerpt 1b — Evidence-first compression
> "This was the first indication that tumours can commandeer the survival function of autophagy to promote tumorigenesis."

After a paragraph of escalating data, this single sentence converts observation into concept. The verb "commandeer" carries interpretive weight without being imprecise.

### Excerpt 1c — Calm judgment under uncertainty
> "The gradual picture that is emerging is that autophagy is required to suppress p62 accumulation and inappropriate activation of NRF2, and perhaps other oncogenic signalling pathways, which can promote survival and tumorigenesis."

"The gradual picture that is emerging" signals a field-level synthesis that is still provisional. "And perhaps other oncogenic signalling pathways" adds a prediction without overclaiming.

### Excerpt 1d — Inference-marking with logical chain
> "This suggests that oncogene activation may be a stress that requires autophagy induction to maintain homeostasis, and, therefore, that RAS activation restricts stress adaptation."

The double construction ("suggests … may be … and, therefore, that …") makes the reasoning transparent. The reader can see exactly where evidence ends and interpretation begins.

### Excerpt 1e — Data-to-concept in a single pass
> "Autophagy deficiency almost abrogates the tumorigenicity of oncogenic RAS-expressing human and mouse cancer cell line models, suggesting that RAS activation and autophagy deficiency are synthetically lethal."

"Almost abrogates" is precisely calibrated. "Synthetically lethal" is introduced as a logical consequence of the data, not as a borrowed framework imposed on it.

### Excerpt 1f — Understated closure
> "The answers to these and many other interesting questions seem set to maintain the current interest in autophagy and its links to tumour development and metabolism, with the promise of using this information to improve cancer treatment."

No triumphalism. "Seem set to maintain the current interest" is deliberately modest — it projects durability of the research question, not certainty of imminent breakthrough.

---

## Exemplar 2 — Herzig & Shaw 2018

### Excerpt 2a — The protagonist reveal
> "Eukaryotes have evolved a very elegant system that allows them to modulate their metabolism based on nutrient availability; the key player in this system is AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK)."

Build context first, then name the molecule. The semicolon creates a dramatic pause before the reveal. AMPK is not named until the second paragraph of the paper.

### Excerpt 2b — Conceptual compression via metaphor
> "AMPK and mTOR are both components of ancient conserved pathways that have evolved as a yin-yang-like antagonistic mechanism controlling catabolism and anabolism."

Packages a major signalling paradigm into one sentence using a familiar cultural image. This is compression that the reader remembers.

### Excerpt 2c — The sufficiency argument
> "Surprisingly, AMPK activation through direct small-molecule activators, in the absence of mitochondrial damage, is sufficient to induce mitochondrial fission. This finding established AMPK as a crucial and direct regulator of mitochondrial dynamics."

"Surprisingly" marks a genuine conceptual update. The follow-up pronounces the verdict with authority ("established … as"). The necessity-then-sufficiency structure is the gold standard of causal argument in biology.

### Excerpt 2d — Restrained speculation
> "The intricate relationship between AMPK and mitochondria is probably underestimated. As a master regulator of energy homeostasis, AMPK is very likely to target more aspects and functions of mitochondria."

"Probably underestimated" and "very likely" stake a claim about the future of the field without overcommitting. This is how a review signals research direction without editorializing.

### Excerpt 2e — The translational pivot with a concrete number
> "Given that metformin is a potent AMPK activator, these questions are not only important in understanding fundamental biological processes but also have real-life applications for the more than 150 million people worldwide who take metformin."

The shift from molecular biology to public health is achieved in one sentence. The specificity of "150 million" is what makes it land.

### Excerpt 2f — The title callback as summative verdict
> "AMPK therefore acts as a guardian of the mitochondria. This is an ancient and conserved role of AMPK, with connections between AMPK and mitochondria occurring in yeast, Caenorhabditis elegans and flies."

"Guardian" reappears from the title to close the argument. The phylogenetic sweep (yeast → worms → flies) conveys deep evolutionary conservation in one sentence.

### Excerpt 2g — Making open questions vivid
> "It remains to be established whether the strength and/or duration of the signal governs the AMPK response, from a rescue effort to a dismantling and rebuilding mission."

"Rescue effort" vs. "dismantling and rebuilding mission" — narrative language that makes an abstract signalling question tangible.

---

## Exemplar 3 — Giacomello et al. 2020

### Excerpt 3a — Structural parallelism as argument
> "The twofold role of mitochondria as cellular powerhouses and signalling organelles is paralleled by the fact that they are surrounded by two membranes: an inner mitochondrial membrane (IMM) and an outer mitochondrial membrane (OMM), characterized by different composition and function."

The sentence's syntax mirrors its content — two roles, two membranes. Form carries meaning.

### Excerpt 3b — Evolutionary reframing in a single sentence
> "Originally the barrier between the invading endosymbiont and the hostile cytosol environment of the host cell, the OMM evolved into a platform for signalling events that use mitochondria."

Compresses deep time into a syntactic pivot ("barrier → platform"), giving the reader a before/after without a lecture.

### Excerpt 3c — Calibrated uncertainty
> "This latter effect, however, remains contentious, raising questions on its contribution to the mitochondrial fragmentation observed on FIS1 overexpression."

"Remains contentious" is more precise than "is debated" — it implies active disagreement among experts, not mere ignorance.

### Excerpt 3d — Flagging genuine surprise
> "The discovery that mitochondrial shape is embedded in systemic signalling was surprising, because the observed transcriptional modifications and systemic signals occurred before any detectable mitochondrial dysfunction, a finding recapitulated in other settings where FGF21 was produced before mitochondrial function was affected."

"Surprising" is earned by the specific reason that follows. The replication across settings ("recapitulated") reinforces that this is not a one-off anomaly.

### Excerpt 3e — Teleological shorthand
> "The possibility to adopt both turns is therefore an economical solution to a protein which participates in both fusion and cristae remodelling."

"Economical solution" expresses structure–function logic compactly: one protein, two conformations, two jobs. This attribution of "economy" to molecular evolution is characteristic of expert biological prose.

### Excerpt 3f — Graduated commitment
> "All in all, we think that senescence triggers mitochondrial fusion, at least in some specific senescence pathways or tissue contexts. However, it is unclear if this process is required for senescence and whether it is of any relevance in vivo."

Three-step epistemic ratchet: "we think" (opinion) → "at least in some" (scoping) → "whether it is of any relevance in vivo" (challenge). A hallmark of Nature Reviews prose.

### Excerpt 3g — Specifying what evidence is missing
> "However, this model lacks formal proof, for example, in heterologous models such as bacteria."

Not "more work is needed." The authors name the exact experiment (heterologous expression in bacteria) that would settle the question. A productive caveat, not a placeholder one.

---

## Exemplar 4 — Ge et al. 2022

### Excerpt 4a — Paradigm-shift framing
> "This traditional view of copper as solely an active site cofactor has been challenged by emerging evidence that copper is a dynamic signalling metal and metalloallosteric regulator that governs and coordinates biological activities in response to external stimuli."

The "traditional view … has been challenged by" frame — a signature Nature Reviews move for marking a paradigm shift without dismissing the old consensus.

### Excerpt 4b — Axiomatic framing with quantitative compression
> "A central tenet regarding elemental nutrients is that they can be neither created nor destroyed, and like all metals, the total pool of intracellular copper is divided into two subsets: a tightly bound protein pool (micromolar) and a bioavailable labile pool (subfemtomolar)."

The word "tenet" elevates a fact to a first principle. Parenthetical concentration units give precision without interrupting the sentence flow.

### Excerpt 4c — Precise definition of a coined term
> "'Cuproplasia' is defined as copper-dependent cell growth and proliferation. This term encompasses both neoplasia and hyperplasia, describes both primary and secondary effects of copper via signalling pathways and includes both enzymatic and non-enzymatic copper-modulated activities."

First a minimal definition (one sentence), then an extensional specification using three "both…and" pairs. Compressed, yet nothing is ambiguous.

### Excerpt 4d — Classical metaphor for translational significance
> "These findings chart new ground by defining copper bioavailability as a metabolic Achilles heel that can be exploited to target multiple nodes of vulnerability in oncogenic signalling pathways."

"Achilles heel" and "chart new ground" convert a complex pharmacological insight into a single vivid image. Authoritative but not dry.

### Excerpt 4e — The concessive pivot
> "Although Menkes disease and Wilson diseases are rare, these disturbances in copper metabolism have firmly established the contribution of this metal to critical aspects of cellular pathophysiology."

"Although X is rare, it has firmly established Y." Acknowledges a limitation (rarity) and immediately converts it into a strength (proof of principle).

### Excerpt 4f — Paradigm generalization by analogy
> "We propose that cuproplasia is one example of different forms of metalloplasia, as other metals, such as iron, can also regulate signalling pathways to promote iron-dependent cell proliferation (ferroplasia), akin to iron-dependent cell death (ferroptosis)."

The novel term (cuproplasia) is immediately embedded in a family of concepts (metalloplasia), using the well-known ferroptosis as an anchor. Nesting the novel within the established.

### Excerpt 4g — Visionary closing sentence
> "Cuproplasia offers a new lens to view an ancient vulnerability that cancer is, and efforts to target copper-dependent signalling and cell proliferation have the potential to change the natural history of cancer and other diseases."

"A new lens" + "an ancient vulnerability" — the juxtaposition of new/ancient creates intellectual drama. "Change the natural history of" is a clinical-medicine idiom reserved for the final sentence.

### Excerpt 4h — The field's own idiom as evidence vehicle
> "Clinical trials point to the promise of copper depletion on the metastatic microenvironment by rendering the 'soil' of distant tissues less congenial to 'seeds' of primary tumours."

The seed-and-soil hypothesis (Paget, 1889) is embedded inside clinical trial data — using scare quotes to flag the borrowed framework. The sentence speaks in the field's own conceptual tradition while reporting new results.

---

## Shared warning

Do **not** transfer any of these phrases mechanically into new output. Transfer only the underlying habits:

- early conceptual stabilization before complexity
- compression after evidence, not before
- definitional precision with immediate scope-bounding
- calm, purposeful paragraph entry
- judgments calibrated to evidence strength
- transitions driven by logic, not connector words
- conclusions that sharpen open questions rather than dissolving into optimism
